headline: Custer's Command Buried After Little Bighorn, 1876
body: The sun beat down, a relentless hammer on the Montana prairie. Two days had passed since the roar of battle died, since Custer and his command met their end. Now, on June 28, 1876, the living arrived to confront the dead. Major Marcus Reno’s weary men, joined by General Alfred Terry and Colonel John Gibbon’s Montana column, stumbled upon the scene. It was a sight to curdle the blood, a tableau of horror etched under a merciless sky. The air hung thick with the stench of death, a sickly-sweet perfume of decay that clung to every breath. Three days of scorching sun had done its work. Bodies, bloated and black, lay scattered across the ridges and ravines, many twisted into grotesque parodies of life. Mutilations were widespread, a testament to the savagery of the fight and the grim customs of war. Heads crushed, limbs severed, bellies disemboweled. Strong men, hardened by campaign and hardship, turned away, stomachs heaving. Some vomited onto the parched earth, others wiped away tears that cut clean streaks through the dust and grime on their faces. The task was simple, yet monstrous: bury the fallen. But tools were scarce. A handful of spades, perhaps. Most worked with what they had, with bayonets, with bare hands, scraping at the unyielding soil. Shallow graves were the best they could manage, mere depressions in the earth, offering scant cover from the scavengers already circling in the vast, empty sky. Lieutenant Colonel George Armstrong Custer, the flamboyant leader, received a burial eighteen inches deep. A luxury compared to the others. Officers’ graves were marked with stakes, a rolled piece of paper bearing a name slipped inside a spent cartridge and pounded into the wood. A map would record their temporary resting places. But the privates, the rank and file, they were not so lucky. Their final sleep was mostly unmarked, their names lost to the wind. No time for ceremony, no time for dignity. The wounded, those who had survived Reno’s desperate stand, cried out for aid. The nearest hospital was five hundred miles away, a journey across unforgiving land and treacherous rivers. The army had to move, and move fast. The dead would wait. Colonel John Gibbon, surveying the scene, captured the desolation with stark precision. He saw the field, he said, “…as thickly lying in all conceivable positions and dotted about on the ground in all directions are little mounds of freshly turned earth showing where each brave soldier sleeps his last sleep.” But that sleep was not undisturbed. Predators, emboldened by the silence that followed the battle, soon scattered the hastily interred remains. The battlefield, once a scene of carnage, became a charnel house, gleaming with tens of thousands of bones, human and equine, under the indifferent sun. The work was not done. The earth would claim its own, but the memory, like the scattered bones, would linger, a grim reminder of the price of empire on the wild frontier.
location: Little Bighorn River, Big Horn County
